seo.title: Prachinburi Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Prachinburi Province: Thap Lan & Pang Sida National Parks (UNESCO), Sa Morakot ancient site, giant river prawns, and 7 districts to explore. Area: 4,762 km², population: 500,607. Plan your visit.

provinceNameThai: ปราจีนบุรี
overview: Prachinburi is a largely forested province in eastern Thailand, located roughly 130 km east of Bangkok. It is best known as a gateway to two UNESCO-listed national parks — Thap Lan and Pang Sida — which together with Khao Yai and Ta Phraya form the Dong Phayayen–Khao Yai Forest Complex, Thailand's largest protected land area. The province capital sits on the Prachinburi River and holds important Dvaravati and Khmer archaeological sites. Prachinburi is popular for eco-tourism, freshwater fishing, and the famous giant river prawns (kung mae nam) harvested from its rivers.
geography: Prachinburi covers 4,762 km² on the western edge of the Sankamphaeng Range, which forms a natural border with Nakhon Ratchasima to the north. The terrain transitions from river plains around the capital to dense highland forest in the north and east. The Prachinburi River drains much of the province before meeting the Bang Pakong River system to the south. Forests dominate roughly 60% of the province, sheltering elephants, gibbons, hornbills, and over 300 bird species.
highlights: Thap Lan National Park — part of the UNESCO Dong Phayayen–Khao Yai Forest Complex with waterfalls, grasslands, and jungle wildlife
Pang Sida National Park — dramatic waterfalls, butterfly meadows, and some of Thailand's finest bird-watching terrain
Sa Morakot (Green Pool) Archaeological Site — ancient Buddhist monuments and a sacred laterite pool from the 9th–13th century Dvaravati-Khmer era
Si Mahosot Ancient City — Dvaravati-era ruins including the largest known carved Buddha footprint in Thailand
Kabin Chaloem Rat Park — seasonal bird-watching for Siberian grebes migrating October to May
Giant river prawns (kung mae nam) — celebrated for their size and sweetness, best eaten grilled at riverside restaurants along the Prachinburi River
Proximity to Bangkok — just 2–3 hours by road, making the province an ideal weekend nature escape from the capital

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool, dry season with temperatures of 20–30°C. Ideal for trekking in national parks, waterfall visits, and wildlife spotting. Road access to Thap Lan's interior trails is at its best.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Hot season with temperatures reaching 35–38°C. National parks are less crowded. Waterfalls diminish in volume but trails are clear and passable.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Monsoon season brings heavy rain, lush greenery, and full waterfalls. Some park trails may close temporarily. The giant prawn season peaks during this wetter period when rivers run high.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the most comfortable trekking weather and best wildlife viewing in the national parks. The rainy season (July–October) rewards with spectacular waterfalls and fresh prawn harvests.

topAttractions.name: Thap Lan National Park
topAttractions.description: Thailand's second-largest national park at 2,235 km², spanning parts of Prachinburi and Nakhon Ratchasima. A core zone of the UNESCO Dong Phayayen–Khao Yai Forest Complex. Features the Thap Lan plateau with open dipterocarp forests, multiple waterfalls (Namtok Thap Lan, Namtok Huai Yai), a reservoir, and abundant megafauna including elephants and wild gaur. Best explored via guided jungle trails from the park headquarters.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB adults / 100 THB children (Thai nationals: 40 THB adults / 20 THB children)

topAttractions.name: Pang Sida National Park
topAttractions.description: An 844 km² national park declared in 1982, famous for the 10-meter-high Pang Sida Waterfall, abundant butterflies, and exceptional bird diversity. Together with Thap Lan, it anchors the eastern flank of the UNESCO forest complex. Accessible from Kabin Buri district; camping and bungalow accommodation are available at the park headquarters.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB adults / 100 THB children (Thai nationals: 40 THB adults / 20 THB children)

topAttractions.name: Si Mahosot Ancient City & Sa Morakot
topAttractions.description: An extensive Dvaravati and Khmer archaeological complex (9th–13th century) featuring laterite and brick temple foundations, the largest known carved Buddha's footprint in Thailand set in natural laterite, and Sa Morakot — a sacred rectangular pool measuring 115 m × 214 m. An on-site exhibition centre explains the province's role as a centre of early Buddhist civilisation. Free admission to the museum; the pool area is part of Wat Morakot grounds.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free (Prachinburi National Museum on same grounds: free)

topAttractions.name: Kabin Chaloem Rat Park
topAttractions.description: A 900-rai (144 ha) municipal park in Kabin Buri district that transforms into one of eastern Thailand's premier bird-watching spots from October to May, when thousands of little grebes (grebes) migrate from Siberia to overwinter on the park's reservoir. Families visit for lakeside walks and picnics year-round.

topAttractions.name: Prachinburi National Museum
topAttractions.description: Located in the provincial capital, this museum presents Prachinburi's rich history spanning Dvaravati, Khmer, and later Siamese periods. Exhibits include bronze Buddhas, inscribed stone tablets, ceramics, and artefacts recovered from Si Mahosot and surrounding archaeological sites. Well curated with English captions.

topAttractions.name: Prachinburi River Prawn Restaurants (Rim Nam)
topAttractions.description: A string of open-air riverside restaurants along the Prachinburi River in the capital serve the province's most celebrated product: giant freshwater river prawns (kung mae nam) grilled over charcoal and served with seafood sauce. These prawns, farmed and wild-caught locally, are noticeably larger and sweeter than those sold in Bangkok. A quintessential stop for any visit to the province.
topAttractions.entranceFee: No entrance fee — prawns typically 400–800 THB per kg depending on size

localFood: Kung Mae Nam Pao — giant freshwater river prawns grilled over charcoal, Prachinburi's signature dish; served whole with sweet seafood sauce at riverside restaurants along the Prachinburi River
Tom Yum Kung Mae Nam — hot and sour soup using locally caught freshwater prawns with lemongrass, kaffir lime, and galangal; richer and more intensely flavoured than the coastal shrimp version
Kaeng Som Pla — sour tamarind fish curry made with freshwater fish from the Prachinburi River, a staple in local households
Kai Yang — grilled marinated chicken with sticky rice and som tam, widely eaten at roadside stalls throughout the province
Khao Tom Prachinburi-style — rice porridge cooked with local herbs and freshwater fish, a popular breakfast at markets in the capital
Khanom Jeen Nam Ya — fresh rice noodles topped with a rich fish-based curry sauce, commonly sold at morning markets in Kabin Buri
culture: Prachinburi has been inhabited since at least the 9th century CE, when it formed part of the Dvaravati Buddhist kingdom. The province later came under Khmer cultural influence, leaving behind the archaeological complex at Si Mahosot. The annual Prachinburi River Festival celebrates the province's river heritage with boat races, fireworks, and riverside food markets. The province is also known as a centre of giant prawn aquaculture, with riverside communities deeply tied to the Prachinburi River ecosystem. Buddhist traditions are strong, with dozens of forest monasteries located within or adjacent to the national parks.
gettingThere: From Bangkok, Prachinburi is approximately 130 km east via Highway 305 or Route 33 — a drive of 2 to 2.5 hours by car. Buses depart regularly from Bangkok's Eastern Bus Terminal (Ekkamai) and take roughly 2.5 to 3 hours. State Railway of Thailand trains run from Hua Lamphong and Bang Sue stations to Prachinburi (Kabin Buri line), taking around 3 to 4 hours. Minivans from Bangkok's Victory Monument and Mo Chit also serve Prachinburi and Kabin Buri towns.
gettingAround: Within Prachinburi, the most practical option is a rental car or motorcycle, particularly for visiting the national parks and archaeological sites which are spread across the province. Songthaew (shared pickup trucks) connect the capital to larger district towns such as Kabin Buri and Si Maha Phot. Motorcycle taxis serve the capital and Kabin Buri for short trips. There is no public transit into Thap Lan or Pang Sida national parks — a private car or organised tour from Bangkok or Kabin Buri is recommended.
travelTips: Rent a car or motorcycle in the capital — national parks and archaeological sites are widely spread and have no public transport links
Book Thap Lan National Park accommodation well in advance for weekends in November to February, the peak season for trekking
Giant river prawn season is year-round but prawns are largest and most abundant in the cooler months (November to January)
The Sa Morakot and Si Mahosot archaeological complex is free and often overlooked — it is historically one of Thailand's most significant Dvaravati sites
Pang Sida waterfall is most impressive after the monsoon rains (October to November); bring waterproof footwear for the trail
Petrol stations are plentiful in the capital and Kabin Buri but sparse inside national park areas — fill up before entering
The province is predominantly Thai-speaking with little English outside hotels; a translation app is useful at local restaurants and markets
